§3.4 Permutation Matrices

Definition 3.4.1.

A matrix is a permutation matrix iff there is exactly one in each of its column and row, and elsewhere.

Following are some observations of permutation matrices

  1. all permutation matrices are square matrices

  2. is also a permutation matrix.

  3. A permutation matrix times a permutation matrix is still a permutation matrix.

  4. There are permutation matrices of .


  5. This is true as, upon transpose, the in each row of is multiplied with the in the corresponding column of .

note to self: left out at page 122, and still need to reformat the inverse section

Example 3.4.2.

(a) If you take powers of a permutation matrix, why is some eventually equal to ? (b) Find a 5 by 5 permutation matrix so that the smallest power to equal is .

Solution.

(a) Since there are only permutation matrices of size , there will eventually be a power that repeats a previously appeared matrix, creating a cycle. Meaning

(b) A simple “shift right one” matrix has a order of rather than . But thinking in terms of cycles directly gives that a cycle of and a cycle of would do the work. So,

would have the highest possible order of .
